preamble: The transformation from a voiced aspirated bilabial plosive ([bʱ]) to a voiceless labiodental fricative ([f]) is a documented historical sound change, primarily observed in the development of Proto-Indo-European (PIE) into certain daughter languages, notably Latin. This shift involves a series of phonetic alterations, including devoicing, fricativization (lenition), deaspiration, and a slight forward shift in the place of articulation.
phoneticEffects: This shift encompasses devoicing (from voiced to voiceless), fricativization (from a plosive to a fricative), deaspiration (loss of aspiration), and a minor place of articulation shift from bilabial to labiodental

examples.note: The PIE root *bʰer- (to carry, bear) became 'fero' (I carry/bear) in Latin.
The PIE word for 'brother', *bʰrā́tēr, evolved into 'frāter' in Latin.
